f: Three components of Azure API Management
b: API gateway (data plane), management plane, developer portal.

f: APIM tier for multi-region deployment, VNet injection, availability zones
b: Premium (Premium v2 for injection in v2 family).
Serverless, per-call billing, scale to zero, no VNet injection — great for spiky/lightweight APIs.
Versions = breaking changes, consumer-visible. Revisions = non-breaking iterations; test with ;rev=N, then make current.
inbound → backend → outbound, plus on-error.
Global → Workspace → Product → API → Operation; composed with <base />.
rate-limit = short-term burst throttling (429). quota = long-term call/bandwidth cap per renewal period.
Signature via OpenID config JWKS, issuer, audience, expiry, required claims.
Bundles of APIs with visibility, terms, subscription requirement/approval, and product-scope policies.
Consumer identification/analytics; pair with OAuth 2.0 JWT validation for real authentication.
Key-value store for policies; can reference Key Vault secrets.
Front Door/App GW (WAF) public edge → internal APIM → private backends.
Containerized APIM gateway run on-prem/other clouds; Azure keeps the management plane — hybrid/multicloud APIs.
Federated API management: teams own APIs/products with isolated workspace gateways in one instance.
Gateway acquires an Entra token with its managed identity to call the backend — no stored secrets.
Reusable backend config: credentials, mTLS certs, circuit breaker rules, load-balanced pools.
GitOps for APIM: extractor + publisher pipelines promote configuration across environments via Git PRs.
Outbound VNet integration to reach private backends (injection requires Premium/Premium v2).
llm-token-limit (token quotas), llm-semantic-cache (semantic response reuse) for LLM backends.
API-first development: return stub responses before the backend exists.
Full features but NO SLA — never for production.
Queue = point-to-point, competing consumers. Topic = pub/sub via independent filtered subscriptions.
At-least-once: message locked; complete/abandon/dead-letter/defer; lock expiry → redelivery → idempotent handlers required.
At-most-once: fastest, but messages lost on consumer crash.
MaxDeliveryCount exceeded, TTL expired, filter evaluation errors, explicit dead-lettering.
FIFO ordering + stateful processing for messages sharing SessionId; required for strict ordering.
Dedupes by MessageId within a time window — idempotent enqueue for producer retries.
Dedicated MUs, private endpoints, zone redundancy, Geo-DR, CMK, 100 MB messages, JMS 2.0.
Classic Geo-DR replicates METADATA ONLY — messages are not copied to the secondary.
Store large payload in Blob Storage; send a small reference message instead.

f: Service Bus vs Event Grid vs Event Hubs
b: SB = enterprise messages/commands. Event Grid = discrete reactive events (push). Event Hubs = high-throughput event streaming.
>80 GB queue storage, simple semantics, lowest cost — no sessions/dedup/ordering needed.
Azure Service Bus Data Owner / Data Sender / Data Receiver — scope to namespace or entity; use with managed identity.
Scheduled = visible at future time (producer-set). Deferral = consumer sets aside; retrieve by sequence number.
Correlation = fast exact-match on properties. SQL = flexible expressions, higher cost.
Queue buffers producer bursts so consumers process at a sustainable steady rate.
Multiple workers receive from one queue for horizontal scale; each message to one worker.
Distributed transaction = sequence of local transactions + compensating actions, coordinated via messages.
Automatically chain queue/subscription to another entity — fan-in and decoupling topologies.
AMQP 1.0 (HTTPS fallback for some operations).
Security principal + role definition + scope.
Management group → subscription → resource group → resource; assignments inherit downward.
Actions = control plane (ARM). DataActions = data plane (blob contents, queue messages…).
Subtracts from Actions — NOT a deny; other assignments can still grant those ops.
Explicit deny that wins over grants; created by Azure (deployment stacks, managed apps), not directly by users.
Both manage everything; only Owner can assign roles.
Union of all role assignments minus deny assignments.

f: Entra ID roles vs Azure RBAC
b: Separate planes: directory administration vs Azure resources. Global Admin can elevate to UAA at root '/' if needed.
System: 1:1, dies with resource. User: standalone, shareable, survives deletion, pre-provisionable.
Just-in-time role activation: eligibility, approval, MFA, time-bound, access reviews, full audit.
Attribute-based filters on role assignments (e.g., blob tags) — finer than role+scope alone.
AssignableScopes controls where the role can be assigned.
RBAC = who can act. Policy = what configurations are allowed regardless of who.
Deny, Audit, Append, Modify, DeployIfNotExists, AuditIfNotExists, Disabled.
CanNotDelete (edit ok, no delete) and ReadOnly (no changes); apply to critical prod resources.
Hierarchy above subscriptions to inherit Policy + RBAC — backbone of landing zones.
Deprecated — use template specs + deployment stacks.
Narrow data roles > Contributor; lowest scope; assign to groups; PIM for privileged; audit regularly.
External workloads (GitHub Actions, other clouds, K8s) exchange their OIDC tokens for Entra tokens — no stored secrets.
Stateful; priority 100–4096, lowest first, first match wins; service tags and ASGs simplify rules.
Application security group: label NICs by workload so NSG rules reference groups, not IPs.
Non-transitive. Spoke↔spoke traffic must route via hub NVA/firewall (UDRs) or direct peering.
Azure Firewall/NVA, VPN/ER gateways, Bastion, DNS/Private Resolver — shared services.
UDR 0.0.0.0/0 → firewall private IP on workload subnets.
VPN: IPsec over internet, cheap, fast setup. ER: private circuit, SLA, up to 100 Gbps; pair with VPN failover.
Private IP NIC mapped to a specific PaaS instance; on-prem reachable; lets you disable public access; needs private DNS zone.
Subnet-to-service backbone path; service stays public; no on-prem reach; simpler/cheaper, weaker isolation.
privatelink.blob.core.windows.net linked to VNets so the FQDN resolves to the private IP.
Managed inbound/outbound endpoints for hybrid DNS — replaces DNS forwarder VMs.
L4 regional: Azure LB. L7 regional: App Gateway (WAF). L7 global HTTP: Front Door. DNS/global any-protocol: Traffic Manager.
Global anycast L7: CDN caching, TLS offload, WAF, path routing, multi-region origin failover; Premium adds Private Link origins.
Priority, weighted, performance, geographic, subnet, multivalue — DNS-based, protocol-agnostic.
Adds TLS inspection, IDPS, URL filtering, web categories over Standard.
Browser-based RDP/SSH to VMs without public IPs.
Scalable predictable outbound SNAT; prevents port exhaustion.
Managed global transit: branches (SD-WAN/VPN), ER, P2S, hub firewalls, any-to-any routing.
Infrastructure (free, always-on), Network Protection (VNet-level plan), IP Protection (per-IP).
